title: Pre recorded Lecture Part B Recording
body:
Dr Linh Nguyen, Course Convenor Lecturer UNSW Centre for Social Impact Email: linh-nguyen@unsw.edu.au   (Academic) research is typically focused on understanding a specific phenomenon or testing a particular theory, while action research is focused on addressing a specific problem or issue that is occurring in a particular context, such as a business

Based on Bell, Harley, and Bryman (2022); Brannick and Coghlan (2007); Coughlan and Coghlan (2023)   (Academic) research Action Research Goals • Expands knowledge and understanding • Finds solutions to real-world problems. within a specific field. Aims at theoretical Aims to develop practical applications contributions and building a broader body of existing knowledge or theory of knowledge. • Tackles immediate challenges and • Academic research lays the groundwork for improves our everyday live future discoveries, Focus • Conceptual and theoretical. • Practical and solution-oriented. • Often explores broad questions or • Focuses on specific issues or phenomena. challenges within a particular context. Outcomes and • Publishes findings in academic journals or • Produces solutions or Dissemination conferences. recommendations that can be • Contributions are measured by citations implemented directly. and influence on future research. • Success is measured by the effectiveness of the intervention in solving the real-world problem.

Source: Based on Ulvik et al. (2018); Coughlan and Coghlan (2023) 3  Study the complex environment for potential solutions

Conduct in-depth research on environmental, social, and cultural contexts

Evaluate possibilities and provide recommendations to clients/sponsors

Gather and synthesize information, including stakeholder input

Assess feasibility and constraints  • Define a specific question • Quantitative • Literature review (survey) vs. • Determine data needed Qualitative (interview, • Research methods observations …) • Work plan (who, what, where, how, when) • Create deadlines Plan/ Data Research Design Collection

Conclusions/ Data Share/ Analysis/ Impact Findings • What should be improved •What are the key • How to improve opportunities/ challenges • What resources are •What are strengths/ needed limitations?

Coughlan and Coghlan (2023)   42% No Market need: important not only to understand the business model, but also the users needs. We want to solve real problems in real lives.

Source: Design in Tech Report, 2017   “70% to 80% of new product development that fails does so not for lack of advanced technology, but because of a failure to understand users’ needs.” Eric Von Hippel  Data  Data  “Garbage in is garbage out” The quality of output is determined by the quality of the input

Secondary data: Extracted Primary data: Collected for the from existing sources specific purpose at hand • Databases • Interviews • Industry reports, country • Surveys reports etc. • Government data: ABS, • Observation Austrade, • Meetings or focus groups • Company documents • Etc • Etc

What do we need What do we What do we still How could we to know? already know? need to find out? find out?

“Great thinkers ask great questions” (Hans-Georg Gadamer)  What are the key advantages? What are the key disadvantages & ▪ Save time, expense and effort risks? ▪ Secondary data helps refine primary data ▪ Secondary data, collected by other collection by identifying gaps and needed researchers for different purposes, supplementary information. may not align with your specific ▪ Data from reliable sources, like the World Bank, research needs. can be more robust than personally collected ▪ Accuracy of secondary data at data due to their extensive resources for times is not known. thorough analyses. ▪ Data may be outdated. ▪ Access to secondary data allows comparisons across multiple locations or organizations that would be difficult to collect independently.

Qualitative: Stories, opinions, explanations (why? Quantitative: Closed questions; Information you how?), ideas, reasons for certain opinions, etc. want to be able to quantify (e.g. X% of people asked say Y; The majority of people prefer the Examples: colour red over blue). ▪ Do you prefer A or B? WHY? Can you explain to me how this industry is structured? If a new Examples: ingredient is offered to you, how is it evaluated ▪ Do you prefer A or B? and on what grounds? What do you think of the current government in Australia? ▪ How many times in the past 4 weeks did you travel by train? ▪ “We tend to have an image of a country. We project this image on the products that ▪ To what extend to you agree with the come from that country, e.g. New Zealand following statements: Products made in is “fresh”; Germany is “technically strong”. Australia tend to be of good quality (scale of When a product label says “made in 1 – 5 Agree or disagree) Australia” what words come to mind?”

Qualitative: • “Purposive” or “convenience”? • Who would know? Who can you get access to? • Everyone knows something and everyone knows someone Quantitative: • Who? What characteristics will influence responses (age, sex, income, address, faith, • habits)? • Which segment of all the possible respondents do you want to focus on? • How will you get access (is it possible?) • How many? Statistically 100 respondents per segment is considered a minimum for general market research though the exact sample size depends on specific research requirements, desired confidence level, and margin of error. Evaluating resources - ELISE | Informing your studies tutorial - Library guides at UNSW Library 20  Academic Sources, for example, Peer-reviewed journal articles, University press books (e.g., Oxford University Press, Cambridge University Press), Books from reputable Publishers (e.g. SAGE Publications, Routledge)

Government, International Organisation Official Reports, for example, Australian Bureau of Statistics (ABS), Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD), World Health Organization (WHO), United Nations (UN, World Bank publications, etc

Professional & Industry Publications such as McKinsey & Company reports, Industry-specific white papers, etc

Educational Institutions such as resources from reputable universities, open-access academic repositories (e.g., JSTOR, ResearchGate)

Reputable News Outlets, for example, The Economist, Forbes, etc

▪ Bell, E., Harley, B. and Bryman, A., 2022. Business research methods. Oxford university press. ▪ Brannick, T. and Coghlan, D., 2007. In defense of being “native”: The case for insider academic research. Organizational Research Methods, 10(1), pp.59-74. ▪ Coughlan, P. and Coghlan, D., 2023. Action research. In Research Methods for Operations and Supply Chain Management (pp. 219-251). Routledge. ▪ Ulvik, M., Riese, H. and Roness, D., 2018. Action research–connecting practice and theory.
